burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery (Ancient Burying Ground), Farmington, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: Farmington militia captain his entire life
narrative_blocks.body: He held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut militia and was known by that title for the rest of his life. He spent his entire life in Farmington — born there February 15, 1704/5 and dying there December 19, 1764, in his 60th year.

narrative_blocks.header: The big chair — militia saluted him sitting
narrative_blocks.body: In later years he became so corpulent he could not attend to business. On fine days a large chair was placed in the front yard near the door, where he sat smoking his pipe and visiting with passersby. When the local militia paraded, it was customary for them to march around by his house and salute him in the big chair.

narrative_blocks.header: Heir to the famous Farmington Hooker Elm
narrative_blocks.body: He was the son of Hon. John Hooker — the Farmington judge whose famous Hooker Elm gave the town its hospitality tradition. His grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker had ministered Farmington's church for thirty years; his great-great-grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker founded Hartford.

sources.short: Connecticut Town Marriage Records pre-1870 (Barbour Collection)
Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT

burial.plot_notes: Find a Grave memorial — Memento Mori Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Her second cousin Martha married her husband's older brother Ezekiel.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-six leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail married James E. Cowles in August 1753 — same Farmington family network that produced her cousin Martha's marriage to James's older brother Ezekiel a decade earlier. She bore seven children between 1754 and 1771 and died at thirty-six in February 1772, eighteen months before her husband remarried Mary Bull.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her cousin married Cowles brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail and her second cousin Martha Hooker Cowles married brothers — Abigail to James E. Cowles in 1753, Martha to Ezekiel Cowles a decade earlier. Both Hooker cousins descended from Rev. Samuel Hooker through different sons; both Cowles brothers were sons of Capt. Isaac Cowles by his third wife Elizabeth Smith.

sources.short: Connecticut Town Marriage Records pre-1870 (Barbour Collection)
Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT
Genealogy of the Cowles family in America (Cowles, 1929)
sources.notes: James E. Cowles is entry #118 in Cowles Genealogy.

marriages.spouse_name: Mary Bull Cowles (1736-1811) — built as X02856 in Stage 6Y-189 per new spouse-entry policy.

parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Smith Cowles (1689-1767), Capt. Isaac Cowles's third wife, not built per Jalapeño Rule.

military_service.notes: Appointed Ensign May 1771; promoted Lieutenant May 1772 of the 1st Company / Trainband, 15th Connecticut Militia. Per Cowles Genealogy entry #118.

career.title: Inheritor of Capt. Isaac Cowles homestead
career.notes: Inherited from his father the place at the south end of Farmington Street (occupied around 1850 by James W. Cowles, Cowles Genealogy entry #743 — not built).

narrative_blocks.header: Brothers both married Hooker-side second cousins
narrative_blocks.body: James and his older brother Ezekiel both married women who were second cousins to each other through their Hooker line — Ezekiel to Martha Hooker in 1748, James to Abigail Hooker in 1753. The brothers' children grew up first cousins on the Cowles side and third cousins on the Hooker side.

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant promotion came same month wife died
narrative_blocks.body: He was promoted from Ensign to Lieutenant of the 1st Company Trainband 15th Connecticut Militia in May 1772 — the same month his wife Abigail Hooker Cowles died. The personal-and-public events coincided in the heaviest weeks of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost first wife 1772, remarried widow Mary Bull
narrative_blocks.body: Eighteen months after Abigail Hooker Cowles's February 1772 death, he married Mary Bull on 15 November 1773 — widow of William Lewis. The new wife brought stepchildren from her first marriage into the Farmington Cowles household.

sources.short: Memorial History of Hartford County, Connecticut (Trumbull ed., 1886) — Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles
Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #614 Diadema Cowles Deming

narrative_blocks.header: Five of eight children died as infants
narrative_blocks.body: She and Chauncey Deming had eight children between 1784 and 1794; five died as infants or young children. The pattern included two infants named Samuel — the second born after the first had died — and three unnamed infants. Only Catharine (b.1787) and the first Samuel survived to adulthood; only Catharine produced descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: Christmas Day birth in Farmington, 1756
narrative_blocks.body: She was born on Christmas Day 1756 at Farmington Hartford County CT — second child of James Cowles and Abigail Hooker Cowles. The birth occurred at the height of the French and Indian War, twenty years before Farmington would send its sons to the Revolutionary muster fields.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife of Farmington's wealthiest 1790-1825 merchant
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Chauncey Deming was the dominant Farmington merchant of his generation, per Trumbull's 1886 Memorial History of Hartford County. She died at Farmington in September 1826 aged sixty-nine, fifteen months before Chauncey himself died in his seventieth year.

narrative_blocks.header: A childless second marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Amy Cowles married the widower Joseph Andrews in 1806 at age 47 and the marriage produced no children. His eight children by his deceased first wife Lydia Judd were her stepchildren, most of them already grown or buried by the time she entered the household.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband was her first cousin via Capt. Isaac Cowles.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin Elijah Cowles
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth and Elijah Jr. shared the same Cowles grandfather Capt. Isaac Cowles and grandmother Elizabeth Smith — Elizabeth's father James and Elijah's father Elijah Sr. were full brothers. Six children resulted; both Augustuses (the first an infant casualty, the second the merchant of J. & A. Cowles) and both Harriets (the first dead at eighteen, the second outliving everyone) carry her replacement-naming impulse.

H00288.prominent_name_reason: Col. Noadiah Hooker — Revolutionary War officer who raised the first Farmington troops and commanded a regiment at Burgoyne's surrender (Saratoga, 1777); 40 years Justice of the Peace; Farmington representative many sessions. "In all relations of life, both public and private, he discharged every duty with credit to himself and benefit to his fellow citizens."

cross_connections.display_label: Bull engraved the Farmington Library bookplate; both on founding committee.
First cousins who served together — Roger enlisted in his company.
Norton served under his command at Peekskill in spring 1777.

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary War Colonel; 40 years Justice of Peace

narrative_blocks.header: Commanded a regiment at Burgoyne's surrender
narrative_blocks.body: His Connecticut regiment fought in the Saratoga Campaign of 1777, where John Burgoyne's British army laid down its arms. The victory turned the war and brought France in on the American side.

narrative_blocks.header: First Farmington troops to reach Boston
narrative_blocks.body: He raised a hundred-man company of Farmington's elite and arrived at Roxbury three weeks before any other Connecticut soldiers. The Colonial Assembly commissioned him captain on arrival.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent an Oneida boy to mission school
narrative_blocks.body: In 1819 he paid for the education of eighteen-year-old Peter Augustus Takonotas at the Cornwall Foreign Mission School. The boy later took Hooker's surname.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace for forty years
narrative_blocks.body: After the war he served as Farmington's Justice of the Peace from the 1780s through the 1820s. The role made him the local arbiter of debt, marriage, and civil disputes for two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Late returning a library book in 1795
narrative_blocks.body: The Farmington Monthly Library, founded that year, recorded 'Col. Noadiah Hooker his shilling' for a late-return fine. Julius Gay's nineteenth-century history pamphlets preserved the entry.

education.school_name: No college noted — rose through militia ranks

military_service.notes: Raised the first troops enlisted at Farmington and marched in command of a company of 100+ men — the very elite of the vicinity. Arrived in Boston (Roxbury) three weeks before any other enlisted men from Connecticut. Commissioned Captain by Colonial Assembly. Commissioned Colonel by State Legislature 1776. Served in several regiments until close of War. Had command of a regiment at the capture of Burgoyne's army (Saratoga, 1777). Facebook genealogy research (2024) confirms: Samuel Woodruff (1734-1816) of Farmington CT served as private in Capt. Jesse Curtiss' company, Col. Noadiah Hooker's regiment, 1777. Also confirmed by Orderly Book of Capt. Ichabod Norton (1777, Peekskill): Norton served "under Colonel Hooker" at Peekskill 1777. Norton was husband of Ruth Strong (H00297), daughter of Ruth Hooker Strong — a Hooker descendant herself. Two branches of the Hooker tree serving together.
military_service.unit: Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment, Connecticut Militia

narrative_blocks.header: She died a month after her father
narrative_blocks.body: Sally Hooker had followed her husband north to Lower Canada, while her father, Colonel Noadiah Hooker, remained in Farmington. The two died thirty-three days apart in 1823, on opposite sides of the border.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Churchyard, Columbia, Richland County, South Carolina

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1796 graduate became prominent Columbia SC lawyer
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker (the second John Hooker in the family) graduated Yale in 1796 and migrated south to become a prominent lawyer in Columbia South Carolina — an unusual destination for a Connecticut Hooker descendant of the post-Revolutionary era. No record explains why he settled in South Carolina specifically; the migration may have run through Yale-network contacts who had established themselves in the Columbia legal community. His Hooker Family Papers covering 1797 and 1802-1815 are preserved at Yale University Library, Manuscripts and Archives, box 2, folder 35.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age forty-one with no children, wife remarried
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker died in 1815 at age forty-one — before reaching the peak of his Columbia legal career and without confirmed surviving children. His wife Mary Ann Chapman (born October 20, 1789 in Columbia South Carolina) remarried in 1818 to Dr. George Washington Glenn; all her subsequent children, Mary Sophia Glenn and John Edward Glenn, were by her Glenn marriage rather than her Hooker one. Mary Ann lived another fifty-seven years, dying in 1872 at age eighty-three.

narrative_blocks.header: Born 1774 Connecticut, buried Newberry SC region
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker was born in 1774 in Connecticut — the second Hooker descendant of this generation to bear the John name, hence the 'No. 2' suffix internal to the genealogical record. He married Mary Ann Chapman of Columbia South Carolina; she would later be buried at Kings Creek Cemetery in Newberry South Carolina with her second husband.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

career.notes: Educated as physician; settled in Middlebury VT with a drug store and landed property. Property was later sold to his cousin Cyrus Porter.
Joined the Farmington Canal office after relocating to New Haven.
Served as Treasurer of the New Haven Savings Bank — likely its first — until his death in 1850.

bio.notes: Named "Griswold" for maternal Griswold ancestry — mother Rebecca Griswold, daughter of Major Josiah Griswold of Wethersfield.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College, tutor + Clerk of the Faculty, 1808-1812 (resigned to marry Elisabeth Daggett).
South Carolina College, tutor c. 1807-1808 (concurrent with private law study under brother John Hooker, practicing lawyer in Columbia SC).

notable.notable_blurb: Old Red College school precursor to Miss Porter's

narrative_blocks.header: Old Red College became Miss Porter's first schoolhouse
narrative_blocks.body: He converted the old Hooker family house — formerly home of a descendant of Ezekiel Cheever, founder of Boston Latin — into the 'Old Red College' preparatory school in Farmington. The building was later demolished to make way for Miss Porter's School's first schoolhouse.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's uncle signed the Declaration of Independence
narrative_blocks.body: While serving as Yale tutor and Clerk of the Faculty (1808-1812), he met Elisabeth Daggett — niece of Roger Sherman, the only Founding Father to sign all four great state papers. He resigned his Yale post in 1812 to marry her.

narrative_blocks.header: His son compiled this very Hooker genealogy
narrative_blocks.body: His son Edward, born Christmas Day 1822, became Commander Edward Hooker U.S.N. In retirement he compiled the foundational 1909 Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker — the primary genealogical source for this entire project.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale tutor who studied law in South Carolina
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale 1805 and traveled to Columbia, South Carolina to study law with his elder brother John, also teaching briefly at Old Ninety Six. Yale appointed him tutor in 1808 — a post he held until 1812 when he returned to Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: Painted by Jared Bradley Flagg around 1840
narrative_blocks.body: His c. 1840 portrait by Jared Bradley Flagg, oil on canvas 30 x 24.5 inches, descended through his great-grandson Joseph K. Hooker. It now hangs at the Harriet Beecher Stowe Center in Hartford.

cross_connections.display_label: Old Red College building site became Miss Porter's first schoolhouse

career.organization: Cambridge SC (Old Ninety Six), South Carolina
career.notes: Brief teaching stint while studying law in South Carolina with brother John.

career.organization: Under brother John Hooker, practicing lawyer at Columbia, South Carolina
career.notes: Edward did not complete legal practice — abandoned law for academia and farming when he returned to CT in 1808.
Concurrent with continuing law study under his brother.
Met Elisabeth Daggett (niece of Roger Sherman, Declaration of Independence signer) through Yale connections; resigned tutorship to marry her.
Returned to Farmington 1812 to manage father's farm and care for father in declining years. Built home for Elisabeth on family land.

career.role: Founder + Master, "Old Red College" private school
career.organization: Farmington CT (in family's former Cheever-descendant house)
career.notes: After his mother's death, Edward brought his father into his own house and converted the old family home into a private collegiate-prep school. The 'Old Red College' fitted many future public figures for college. The house had been previously inhabited by a descendant of Ezekiel Cheever (founder of Boston Latin School and author of the first American school books). Edward later proposed closing his school in favor of a new Farmington Academy under Simeon Hart. The school closed in 1816 when the town opened its own academy in the village center under Simeon Hart.

career.role: Local public affairs + literary work + scientific farming
career.notes: After closing the school, Edward devoted himself to writing, town affairs, and scientific farming until his death.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Owner (1811-1846); 1811 Federal-style house built for him on High Street, Farmington CT. Inherited at his death by son John Hooker (H01626).

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: Abigail died young
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail died in 1775, aged about three. Abigail’s parents gave the name to a child born five years later.

military_service.notes: Lieutenant in the Militia pre-Revolution. Remained neutral during the war through the influence of his Loyalist wife's family.
Active in Farmington town affairs before the war; remained neutral through it (EH #292).

narrative_blocks.header: Neutral in the Revolution, ostracized afterward
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Ingersoll's family were Loyalists; Joseph remained neutral throughout the war. Afterward, while his brother and cousins who had served received honors, Joseph found 'the confidence of the town was entirely withheld from him.'

narrative_blocks.header: Married into a Loyalist Hooker descendant family
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Ingersoll was the daughter of Rev. Jonathan Ingersoll — himself a Hooker descendant through a separate line. The Ingersoll family were committed Loyalists; this marriage united two Hooker-descendant branches across opposing political loyalties.

narrative_blocks.header: Rebuilt his reputation in Litchfield, New York
narrative_blocks.body: He removed to Litchfield in Herkimer County, New York, where he became active and trusted in public affairs. His new neighbors honored his integrity and loyalty to the new nation, restoring the standing he had lost in Farmington.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Cheever (2nd wife of William Lewis III)
parents.father_research_notes: William Lewis III (b. Aug 19, 1620, d. Aug 18, 1690, Farmington). Son of William Lewis the Hartford Founder (b. 1594, d. 1683). First town clerk of Farmington after 1645 incorporation. Captain in King Philip's War 1675. Buried Memento Mori Cemetery.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington. FindAGrave confirms.
